description: The provided image displays a publisher update badge rather than a scientific materials figure. Visually, it consists of a light gray square background containing a circular emblem with concentric blue and gold rings, a red bookmark symbol positioned centrally, and the black text "Check for updates" printed directly below the emblem. No scientific data pertaining to the low-density δ-ferrite steel study—such as scanning electron micrographs, X-ray diffraction patterns, spectroscopy data, or mechanical property curves for the rolled samples R650, R750, R850, and R950—are present in the image pixels. Consequently, microstructural features including ferrite/carbide composite lamellae, spheroidized carbide dispersions, martensite phases, or fracture surface morphologies cannot be observed or verified visually. The scientific content described in the accompanying text extraction summary is not represented in this graphic.

Figure 1 presents the experimental processing route and mechanical testing specimen geometries for the investigated low-density δ-ferrite steel. Panel (a) shows a temperature–time profile comprising initial high-temperature homogenization, holding, forging, and air cooling, followed by reheating to four distinct set points—650 °C, 750 °C, 850 °C, and 950 °C—with a 2 h hold, hot rolling, and final air cooling; citation text states the homogenization was at 1200 °C for 4 h with forging to 40 mm thickness, and identifies the resulting samples as R650, R750, R850, and R950. The Ac1 critical temperature is marked to delineate the carbide precipitation/dissolution regime below ~750 °C from the dynamic recrystallization and deformation-induced ferrite transformation regime at ≥ 850 °C. Panel (b) details the rod-shaped tensile specimen geometry, featuring a 25 mm gauge length and 5 mm diameter, sampled along the rolling direction (RD). Panel (c) illustrates the Charpy V-notch impact specimen with dimensions 10 mm × 10 mm × 55 mm, where the notch is positioned on the rolled surface and the normal direction (ND) is indicated relative to RD. Together, these schematics define the thermomechanical processing windows and standardized specimen designs that underpin the study of microstructure–toughness relationships.

Optical micrographs of a low-density δ-ferrite steel (Fe–4.5Al–1.47Mn–0.35C, wt%) subjected to 60% hot-rolling reduction at (a) 650 °C (R650), (b) 750 °C (R750), (c) 850 °C (R850), and (d) 950 °C (R950), observed on the RD–ND plane with a 50 μm scale bar. Subfigures (a) and (b) depict a continuous banded lamellar structure in the low-temperature rolling zone, where light δ-ferrite lamellae alternate with dark carbide/ferrite composite lamellae; caption states the carbide/ferrite volume fraction decreases from 51.5% to 43.3% upon increasing temperature from 650 °C to 750 °C, consistent with carbide dissolution kinetics. Subfigures (c) and (d) reveal a duplex lamellar architecture in the high-temperature rolling zone after complete carbide dissolution, consisting of light δ-ferrite lamellae interspersed with dark honeycomb martensite lamellae that encapsulate equiaxed ferrite grains (marked by arrows). The figure establishes that rolling temperature governs the fundamental phase architecture, driving a transition from carbide-reinforced composite lamellae to a δ-ferrite/martensite duplex laminate that critically determines deformation coordination and impact toughness.

Figure 3 compiles SEM and TEM micrographs elucidating the regulatory effect of rolling temperature on microstructure in low-density δ-ferrite steel. The as-forged baseline (a–c) displays coarse δ-ferrite with interspersed martensite/austenite (M/A) constituents and no lamellar ordering. After low-temperature rolling at 650 °C (R650; d–f), a distinct lamellar architecture forms, comprising δ-ferrite bands alternating with carbide/ferrite composite lamellae that contain ultra-fine spherical carbides averaging 158.3 ± 76.9 nm. For R750 (g–i), carbides coarsen to 247.3 ± 86.8 nm, exhibiting Ostwald ripening with coarse carbides at grain boundaries and finer particles inside grains. In the high-temperature regime (R850 and R950; j–o), spherical carbides vanish entirely, replaced by honeycomb martensite lamellae embedding equiaxed deformation-induced ferrite grains that grow markedly with increasing temperature. Collectively, these images demonstrate that low-temperature rolling stabilizes ultra-fine spheroidized carbides within ferrite/carbide composite lamellae, whereas high-temperature processing eliminates carbides and fosters brittle martensite lamellae, thereby controlling fracture behavior and impact toughness.

This figure presents a comprehensive EBSD and nanoindentation characterization matrix for low-density δ-ferrite steel processed at four rolling temperatures (R650, R750, R850, R950). The leftmost column (a–d) shows GB + GOS maps, where green regions denote GOS ≤ 2° and black/grey lines mark high-angle (>15°) and low-angle (2–15°) grain boundaries. The KAM maps (e–h) visualize kernel average misorientation (scale 0–4.99°), indicating stored dislocation density. The IPF maps (i–l) reveal grain orientation distributions relative to the rolling direction. The rightmost column (m–p) overlays nanoindentation hardness (NI) onto the microstructure. Visual trends and caption data together show that increasing rolling temperature raises the HAGB fraction from 43.5–49.6 % (low-temperature zone) to 60.1–75.7 % (high-temperature zone) and increases the GOS ≤ 2° proportion from 6.9–12.3 % to 19.4–25.8 %, while KAM values decrease from 0.48–0.55° to 0.3–0.4°, indicating progressive recrystallization and dislocation annihilation. Correspondingly, IPF textures shift from dominant <001> toward <101>. Nanoindentation maps report a small hardness mismatch (~0.72 GPa) between carbide/ferrite composite lamellae and δ-ferrite lamellae at low temperatures, whereas high-temperature processing produces a severe gradient (martensite 5.98–6.50 GPa vs. δ-ferrite 2.64–2.77 GPa, maximum difference 3.86 GPa). These results support the conclusion that low-temperature rolling preserves ultra-fine carbides that pin dislocations via the Orowan mechanism and promote deformation coordination, while high-temperature rolling induces extreme hardness mismatch at phase boundaries, creating preferential sites for crack initiation and impairing toughness.

Figure 5 presents the mechanical response of low-density δ-ferrite steel rolled at 650–950 °C (samples R650, R750, R850, R950). Panel (a) shows room-temperature impact energy dropping sharply from ~207 J (R650) to near-zero values for R850 and R950. Impact load–displacement curves in (b) and (c) reveal that R650 undergoes extensive stable crack propagation (Ep = 147.90 J), R750 exhibits shortened propagation (Ep = 51.51 J, Ei = 50.14 J), while R850 and R950 display abrupt single-peak failure with minimal absorbed energies (Ei ≈ 3–4 J, Ep ≈ 2 J), indicating immediate unstable crack growth. Panel (d) presents engineering stress–strain curves: tensile strength increases monotonically from ~869 MPa (R650) to ~1255 MPa (R950), but elongation collapses from ~19 % to ~3.5 %. Work-hardening rate curves in (e) and (f) show a distinct hump for R650/R750, attributed to Orowan pinning by ultra-fine spheroidized carbides, whereas R850/R950 exhibit monotonic decay, reflecting the loss of coordinated deformation capacity at high rolling temperatures.

Figure 6 presents thermodynamic phase equilibrium calculations for the low-density δ-ferrite steel system. Subfigure (a) displays an iron-carbon equilibrium phase diagram spanning 0.0–1.5 wt.% C and 600–1400 °C, delineating phase regions including BCC, FCC, BCC+FCC, BCC+FCC+θ, and BCC+Carbides, with a vertical dashed line at approximately 0.35 wt.% C marking the alloy composition. Subfigure (b) plots the equilibrium volume fraction of each phase—Carbides (black), BCC (red), and FCC (blue)—as a function of temperature from 550 °C to 1150 °C. The curves reveal that carbides are stable below approximately 730–750 °C, above which they dissolve completely while FCC austenite forms and its fraction increases with temperature, peaking near 1050–1100 °C. Together, these diagrams predict that for the 0.35 wt.% C alloy, rolling within the low-temperature regime (<750 °C) preserves ultra-fine spheroidized carbides in ferrite/carbide composite lamellae, whereas high-temperature rolling (≥850 °C) leads to full carbide dissolution and subsequent formation of δ-ferrite plus martensite lamellae upon cooling, consistent with the observed microstructural transitions in the experimental samples processed at 650 °C, 750 °C, 850 °C, and 950 °C.

Figure 7 presents TEM bright-field images, SAED patterns, and EDS elemental maps for the R650 low-density δ-ferrite steel rolled at 650 °C. Panel (a) gives an overview of elongated ultrafine carbides (scale bar 125 nm), while (b)–(d) resolve individual precipitates ranging from rod-like to spheroidized morphologies (scale bars 125 nm and 50 nm). SAED insets confirm the phases as M₃C ([201]), M₂₃C₆ ([110]), and M₇C₃ (pattern indexed in inset), respectively. Corresponding EDS maps reveal significant Mn, Cr, and C enrichment within the carbide particles, with corresponding Al depletion relative to the matrix. These carbides lie outside the δ-ferrite lamellae and caption reports they precipitate in former martensite regions after martensite fragmentation, promoting nearby ferrite formation. Collectively, the figure furnishes direct evidence that ultrafine M₃C, M₂₃C₆, and M₇C₃ carbides disperse in a ferrite matrix, forming ferrite/carbide composite lamellae that underpin the superior impact toughness of the R650 steel.

This figure presents optical (OM) and scanning electron (SEM) micrographs of the deformed zones near impact and tensile fracture surfaces of low-density δ-ferrite steel R650 processed by low-temperature rolling at 650 °C. Subfigures (a) and (b) are OM images showing crack propagation through alternating δ-ferrite and carbide/ferrite composite lamellae aligned parallel to the rolling direction (RD), with ND indicating the normal direction. Subfigure (c) is an OM image of the tensile fracture deformed zone revealing a serrated morphology with delamination characteristics. Subfigures (d–g) are SEM images of the impact deformed zone: (d) shows pronounced blunting of the crack tip (arrow) upon penetrating the carbide/ferrite lamellae; (e) labels a primary crack and secondary cracks; (f) highlights microvoids and microcracks (arrows) nucleated at carbide/ferrite interfaces; and (g) shows a secondary crack propagating horizontally along the lamellar direction (dashed box). Subfigures (h) and (i) are SEM images of the tensile deformed zone, demonstrating that cracks are confined within δ-ferrite lamellae and propagate parallel to the lamellar orientation (arrows). Collectively, these images demonstrate that ultra-fine spheroidized carbides dispersed within ferrite/carbide composite lamellae effectively blunt crack tips, promote interfacial separation and microvoid nucleation, and force cracks to propagate along longer paths, thereby dissipating substantial energy and enabling superior impact toughness and tensile ductility.

Figure 9 combines transmission electron microscopy (TEM and HRTEM/IFFT) images of the impact-fracture deformed zone with schematic illustrations of impact fracture behavior across low-density δ-ferrite steels processed at different rolling temperatures. Subfigures (a) and (c) are TEM micrographs (scale bars 1 μm) of the R650 specimen (rolled at 650 °C) deformed zone, revealing the lamellar ferrite/carbide composite microstructure. Subfigures (b) and (d) are HRTEM images with corresponding IFFT insets (scale bars 5 nm) that resolve atomic shear and dislocation structures within the R650 deformed zone, providing direct nanoscale evidence of deformation mechanisms. Subfigures (e) and (h) schematically depict the impact fracture process for R650, where ultra-fine spheroidized carbides dispersed in δ-ferrite lamellae promote crack deflection, dislocation pinning via the Orowan mechanism, and crack-tip blunting. Subfigures (f) and (i) illustrate the R750 (750 °C) fracture scenario; caption reports that carbide coarsening and reduced particle density weaken crack deflection and Orowan strengthening, leading to straighter crack propagation and roughly 60 % lower impact absorption energy versus R650. Subfigures (g) and (j) depict fracture for R850/R950 (850–950 °C), where complete carbide dissolution produces a dual-phase structure of δ-ferrite and honeycomb martensite lamellae containing equiaxed deformation-induced ferrite (DIF); this microstructural change causes interfacial stress concentration and provides a “crack propagation shortcut” via DIF, resulting in brittle fracture with impact energy dropping below 10 J. The figure collectively establishes that ultra-fine spherical carbides within ferrite/carbide composite lamellae are essential for superior impact toughness, whereas high-temperature rolling degrades toughness through carbide dissolution and brittle phase formation.
